subject: Physics
solution:
As we know,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\frac{N}{N_0}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n} \\
& n=\text { no. of half life } \\
& N \text { - no. of atoms left } \\
& N_0 \text { - initial no. of atoms } \\
& \text { By radioactive decay law, } \\
& \frac{d N}{d \mathrm{t}}=k N \\
& k-\operatorname{disin} t e g r a t i o n \text { constant } \\
& \therefore \frac{\frac{d N}{d t}}{\frac{d N_0}{d t}}=\frac{N}{N_0}
\end{aligned}
$$
From (i) and (ii) we get
$$
\frac{\frac{d N}{d t}}{\frac{d N_0}{d t}}=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n
$$
or, $\left[\frac{100}{1600}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n \Rightarrow\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^4=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n$
$\therefore n=4$, Therefore, in 8 seconds 4 half life had occurred in which counting rate reduces to 100 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$.
$\therefore$ Half life, $\frac{T_1}{2}=2 \mathrm{sec}$
In $6 \mathrm{sec}, 3$ half life will occur
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left[\frac{\frac{d \mathrm{~N}}{d t}}{1600}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^3 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d \mathrm{~N}}{d t}=200 \text { counts } \mathrm{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
solution:
In positive beta decay a proton is transformed into a neutron and a positron is emitted.
$$
\mathrm{p}^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{n}^0+\mathrm{e}^{+}
$$
no. of neutrons initially was $A-Z$
no. of neutrons after decay $(A-Z)-3 \times 2$ (due to alpha particles) $+2 \times 1$ (due to positive beta decay)
The no. of proton will reduce by 8 . [as $3 \times 2$ (due to alpha particles) $+2$ (due to positive beta decay)] Hence atomic number reduces by 8 .

solution:
${ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He} \rightarrow{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Q}$
power generated $=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{Q}$
where, $\mathrm{N} \rightarrow$ No. of reaction $/ \mathrm{sec}$.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\left(3 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{He}}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{C}}\right) \mathrm{C}^2 \\ & \mathrm{Q}=(3 \times 4.0026-12)\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2 \\ & \mathrm{Q}=7.266 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{\text { power }}{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{5.808 \times 10^{30}}{7.266 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}}=5 \times 10^{42}\end{aligned}$
rate of conversion of ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ into ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}=15 \times 10^{42}$ Hence, $\mathrm{n}=15$

Momentum of γ ray Pγ=hλ=hvc
Total momentum is conserved.

P→γ+P→Nu=0
where P→Nu= momentum of decayed nuclei

⇒hvc=PNu
⇒KE of nuclei

=12Mv2=PNu22M=12Mhvc2
Loss in internal energy =Eγ+KENu
=hv+12Mhvc2
=hv1+hv2Mc2

solution:

Number of half-life in 16 s for element A will be, nA=164=4

Number of half-life in 16 s for element B will be, nB=168=2

Hence, ratio of number of nuclei after 16 s will be, =N0A×12nAN0B×12nB=12×124-2=18=25100

Thus, value of x is 25.

subject: Physics
solution:
Using the equation of radioactive decay law,

-dNdt=λ1N+λ2N  ...(1)

-dNdt=λ1+λ2N  ...(2)

Where,

-dNdt is the rate of decay

λ1, λ2 are decay constant for two different processes

N is the number of atoms.

Now applying the decay law for the combine process, we get

-dNdt=λeffN  ...(3)

Equating equation (1) and (2) we get

λeff=λ1+λ2  ...(4)

Now the half life of the radioactive nucleus is given by,

λ=ln2T  ...(5)

Where, T is the half life of the radioactive nucleus

Using equation (5) equation (4) becomes,

ln2T=ln2T1+ln2T2

1T=1T1+1T2

1T=110+1100=11100

⇒T=10011≈9 s

subject: Physics
solution:
The number of $^{235}\text{U}$ atoms in 47 g is determined using Avogadro's number.
Molar mass of $^{235}\text{U}$ is 235 g/mol, so the number of moles is $n = 47/235$ mol.
The total number of atoms is $N = (47/235) \times 6 \times 10^{23}$.
Since each fission releases 190 MeV, the total energy released is:
$E_{total} = N \times 190 = \frac{47 \times 6 \times 10^{23}}{235} \times 190 = \frac{47 \times 6 \times 190}{235} \times 10^{23}$ MeV.
Calculating the numerator: $47 \times 6 \times 190 = 282 \times 190 = 53580$.
Therefore: $E_{total} = \frac{53580}{235} \times 10^{23} = 228 \times 10^{23}$ MeV, giving $\alpha = 228$.

subject: Physics
solution:
Radioactive decay is a random and spontaneous process, it depends on unbalancing of nucleus. This process is dependent on the law of conservation of charge.

We know that, N=N0e-λt    ...1, Where, N=number of decayed nuclei in the sample at time t and N0=initial number of nuclei.

Hence, total number of undecayed nuclei N0-N

Thus, N0-N=N01-e-λt

This shows that the total number of undecayed nuclei decays exponentially with time. 

Taking log both sides of equation 1

lnN=-λt+lnN0

So, slope =-λ  (reciprocal of mean life time)

Half life time T12=ln2λ

Then, λ×T12=ln2=constant

Hence, (B) and (C) are correct only.

subject: Physics
solution:
For the nucleus $^{209}_{83}Bi$, the number of protons is $Z = 83$ and the number of neutrons is $N = 209 - 83 = 126$.

Mass of $83$ protons $= 83 \times 1.007825 = 83.649475 \, u$

Mass of $126$ neutrons $= 126 \times 1.008665 = 127.091790 \, u$

Total mass of the nucleons $= 83.649475 + 127.091790 = 210.741265 \, u$

The mass defect $\Delta m$ is given by:

$\Delta m = \text{Total mass of nucleons} - \text{Mass of nucleus}$

$\Delta m = 210.741265 - 208.980388 = 1.760877 \, u$

The total binding energy ($BE$) is:

$BE = \Delta m \times 931 \, \text{MeV}$

$BE = 1.760877 \times 931 = 1639.376 \, \text{MeV}$

The binding energy per nucleon is:

$\dfrac{BE}{A} = \dfrac{1639.376}{209} \approx 7.84 \, \text{MeV}$

Answer: $7.84$
